query: dependency injection in angular interview question

competitors.label: Angular Dependency Injection Overview
competitors.selectionReason: This is the authoritative Angular DI overview and the strongest canonical baseline for provider and injector concepts.
competitors.winReason: The official docs remain the canonical reference, but our page now surfaces the real subtree-instance bug and the Optional/Self/SkipSelf follow-up more directly in one interview-shaped answer.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative description of DI concepts, what services are, and how values are provided and injected.
theirGaps: It is broader and less interview-shaped around the most common pitfalls and provider variants.

followUpConfusion: @Optional() says “return null if missing.” @Self() says “only check the current injector.” @SkipSelf() says “start at the parent injector.”

realWorldUseCases: The official guide already grounds DI in maintainability, scalability, testing, and shared services, but our page now foregrounds the concrete subtree-override bug more directly.
actionableExamples: Angular.dev still walks through the concepts and provider mechanics in more depth than our compact trivia answer, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: It covers the provider/inject vocabulary well enough that the conceptual follow-ups remain competitive, but our page now spells out Optional, Self, and SkipSelf more explicitly.

actionableExamples: Add one compact provider-shapes example covering useClass, useExisting, useFactory, and useValue in a single block.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Angular Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd’s Angular interview guide is directly relevant because it highlights dependency injection hierarchy as an interview topic.
competitors.winReason: The GreatFrontEnd guide is broad, but our DI page goes deeper on provider shapes, injector proximity, and the pitfall surface interviewers usually ask about next.
theirStrengths: It frames DI as a core Angular interview topic and keeps the architecture context practical.
theirGaps: It is not as specific about provider shapes, multi providers, or resolution modifiers.

realWorldUseCases: A root-provided service can look “global” until a feature route or component provides the same token again.
followUpConfusion: When Angular needs a dependency, it checks the current injector (e.g., component injector), and if not found it walks up to parent injectors until it finds a provider. If multiple levels provide the same token, the nearest one is used.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd marks DI hierarchy as important, but it stays at the topic-map level rather than unpacking the pitfall-heavy parts of DI resolution.
actionableExamples: Its guide is practical and interview-oriented enough that the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: The guide is lighter on provider-shape mechanics, nearest-provider wins, and multi-provider behavior.

actionableExamples: Add one explicit provider-resolution diagram showing root, route, and component providers for the same token.

competitors.label: Angular University Dependency Injection Guide
competitors.selectionReason: Angular University has a dedicated DI guide, making it a strong tutorial/reference competitor for injector mechanics and provider patterns.
competitors.winReason: Angular University is deeper overall, but our page is now stronger on the concrete provider-scope bug and on the fast interview follow-up around Optional, Self, and SkipSelf.
theirStrengths: It is a topic-specific long-form tutorial that goes deeper than short interview answers normally do.
theirGaps: It is less compact and less directly shaped around a quick interview explanation.

realWorldUseCases: A root-provided service can look “global” until a feature route or component provides the same token again.
followUpConfusion: @Optional() says “return null if missing.” @Self() says “only check the current injector.” @SkipSelf() says “start at the parent injector.”

realWorldUseCases: Angular University is also focused on DI in practice, but our page now opens with the provider-scope bug pattern that many interview answers miss.
actionableExamples: As a dedicated long-form DI tutorial, it still offers more step-by-step examples than our compact trivia page, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Because it is a focused guide, it still answers many DI follow-ups deeply, but our page now covers the key resolution modifiers much more directly.

actionableExamples: Add a short useFactory example with environment-dependent configuration so provider shapes feel less abstract.
